coredata.dc:title: New bioelectrode based on graphene quantum dots-polypyrrole film and Concanavalin A for pathogenic microorganism detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Santos, Maria P.M.C.
de Oliveira, Léony S.
Lima-Neto, Reginaldo G.
Andrade, César A.S.
Oliveira, Maria D.L.
coredata.dc:description: Infections caused by microorganisms are a public health problem worldwide. New biodetection systems are essential to diagnose with accuracy resulting in more effective treatment. In this work, we propose a ConA-conjugated graphene quantum dots and polypyrrole film-based biosensor for label-free detection of Candida albicans, Candida glabrata, Candida tropicalis, E. coli, B. subitilis, and S. aureus. We modified polypyrrole and graphene quantum dots (PPY-QDGs) with Concanavalin A (Con A) lectin. ConA is a glucose/mannose-specific lectin. The results showed that ConA lectin has the highest binding affinity for C. tropicalis and S. subtilis. PPY-GQDs-ConA binding profile revealed differential response for Candida spp (C. tropicalis > C. albicans > C. glabrata) and bacterial (B. subtilis > S. aureus > E. coli). The limits of detection (LOD) obtained were 1.42 CFU/mL for C. albicans, and 3.72 CFU/mL for C. glabrata. C. tropicalis yielded a LOD of 0.18 CFU/mL. The respective LODs for the evaluated bacteria were 0.39 CFU/mL for S. aureus, 0.72 CFU/mL for S. subtilis, and 2.63 CFU/mL for E. coli. The differential response obtained for the sensor can be attributed to the heterogeneous distribution of carbohydrates on the microorganism's surfaces. The proposed system based on a flexible substrate is effective for microbiological diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Concanavalin A
Quantum dots
Microorganisms
Biosensor
Impedance spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Biotechnological application of lectins in microbiological biosensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM images of the stepwise modification process: PPy (a), PPy-GQDs (b), PPy-GQDs-ConA (c), PPy-GQDs-ConA-BSA (d), ConA biosensor - E. coli (e), and ConA biosensor - C. albicans (f).
Cyclic voltammogram (a) and Nyquist diagram (b) of the stepwise modification of the ConA Biosensor.

Electrochemical characterization of Candida spp. biorecognition assays: (a) Cyclic voltammogram and (b) Nyquist diagram for C. albicans, (c) CV and (d) EIS of C. tropicalis and (e) CV and (f) EIS for C. glabrata.

Electrochemical characterization of bacteria biorecognition assays: (a) Cyclic voltammogram and (b) Nyquist diagram for E. coli:, (c) CV and (d) EIS for S. aureus and (e) CV and (f) EIS for B. Subitilis.
Degree of surface coating (θ%) for biorecognition performance of ConA biosensor (a) and three-dimensional plot of Candida spp. (b) and bacteria (c) (n = 3).
Comparison between biotechnological applications for microorganisms detections based in lectins.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Cys: Cysteine. MBA: Mercaptobenzoic acid. AuNps; Gold nanoparticles. CdTe QDs: Cadmium telluride quantum dots. Fe3O4: Magnetite nanoparticles. Chit: Chitosan. ITO: Indium tin oxide. PPy: Polypyrrole. GQDs Graphene quantum dots. ConA: concavalin A. WGA: wheat germ lectin. Cramoll: Cratylia mollis.

head.ce:title: New bioelectrode based on graphene quantum dots-polypyrrole film and Concanavalin A for pathogenic microorganism detection

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Departamento de Bioquímica, UFPE, Recife, Pernambuco 50670-901, Brazil.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Infections caused by microorganisms are a public health problem worldwide. New biodetection systems are essential to diagnose with accuracy resulting in more effective treatment. In this work, we propose a ConA-conjugated graphene quantum dots and polypyrrole film-based biosensor for label-free detection of Candida albicans, Candida glabrata, Candida tropicalis, E. coli, B. subitilis, and S. aureus. We modified polypyrrole and graphene quantum dots (PPY-QDGs) with Concanavalin A (Con A) lectin. ConA is a glucose/mannose-specific lectin. The results showed that ConA lectin has the highest binding affinity for C. tropicalis and S. subtilis. PPY-GQDs-ConA binding profile revealed differential response for Candida spp (C. tropicalis > C. albicans > C. glabrata) and bacterial (B. subtilis > S. aureus > E. coli). The limits of detection (LOD) obtained were 1.42 CFU/mL for C. albicans, and 3.72 CFU/mL for C. glabrata. C. tropicalis yielded a LOD of 0.18 CFU/mL. The respective LODs for the evaluated bacteria were 0.39 CFU/mL for S. aureus, 0.72 CFU/mL for S. subtilis, and 2.63 CFU/mL for E. coli. The differential response obtained for the sensor can be attributed to the heterogeneous distribution of carbohydrates on the microorganism's surfaces. The proposed system based on a flexible substrate is effective for microbiological diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Hospital-acquired infections are obtained in hospital units during or after hospitalization. Criteria for diagnosis include clinical symptoms and laboratory confirmation after 72 hours of hospitalization or, in cases of invasive procedures, within a shorter period [1]. In Brazil, healthcare-associated infections represent the fourth leading cause of death in Intensive Care Units [2].

It is possible to highlight that in the hospital environment, infections can arise from a wide variety of pathogens, and this diversity can vary depending on the region, population served, health systems, and medical facilities in each nation [3]. Some of these microorganisms, such as fungal species and bacteria, can become opportunistic and cause infections, especially in hospital environments where there is a wide variety of pathogens. These healthcare-associated infections are a global concern, affecting patients with different medical conditions [4].

Candida spp. is the most common group of fungi in human infections and is also associated with polymicrobial infections, in which complex interactions occur between microorganisms. An example of interaction in polymicrobial infection can occur when C. albicans produces biofilm, favoring the adhesion and proliferation of bacteria. Synergistic interaction can occur between C. albicans, Streptococcus gordonii, and Staphylococcus aureus, with the yeast facilitating bacterial adhesion and promoting plaque and bacterial caries formation in the oral cavity. These interactions can influence virulence and pathogenicity and affect the host's immune response [5].

The Candida group includes around 150 species, but only 13 of them are harmful to humans, such as C. albicans, C. tropicalis, C. glabrata, and C. krusei. These yeasts can coexist with several bacterial species, such as S. epidermidis, S. aureus, and B. subtilis as Gram-positive group and P. aeruginosa, E. coli, and Enterobacter spp as Gram-negative group [6].

Candida's extracellular matrix is mainly composed of 10 % proteins and 90 % carbohydrates, with polysaccharides found in the cell wall, which include mannans, β-glucans, and chitin, forming an immunological signature. In general, carbohydrates play a predominant role in the process of immune recognition, while proteins play a crucial role in adhesive interactions with host cell surfaces [7].

Delays in diagnosing candidemia and bacteremia are linked to elevated mortality rates [8]. In this context, it is crucial to carry out an accurate and complete diagnosis, including identification of the species involved, which is essential to ensure early and effective treatment. Therefore, traditional diagnostic methods, such as culture, are considered a reference and widely used diagnostic approach. However, identifying yeasts of the genus Candida spp and gram-negative and positive bacteria through cultures is time-consuming, taking two to three days for results. This delay results in less accurate diagnoses and, consequently, delays the implementation of the therapeutic measures necessary to treat infections effectively [9]. The polymerase chain reaction (PCR) technique stands out for its accuracy when differentiating between genotypes. However, it has disadvantages due to the delay, high reagent costs, and the need for specialists to be carried out [10].

Nevertheless, diverse studies have demonstrated the applicability of biosensors as a diagnostic method for microorganisms. Nanostructures associated with electroanalytical techniques, such as electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV), have been extensively used for the development of nanobiosensors [11]. Nanomaterials such as graphene quantum dots, carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, and metallic nanoparticles have common characteristics such as excellent electron transfer ability and electrical conductivity [12]. The special use of quantum dots nanomaterial in electrochemical biosensors can provide a large surface area, high electrochemical activity, and the possibility of functionalization [13]. Graphene quantum dots (QDGs) can provide a natural environment for the immobilization of biomolecules and facilitate electron transfer due to their large surface area, electrochemical properties, and good biocompatibility [14]. The combination of electrochemical transduction techniques and nanostructuring with biocompatible materials having active bioelectrochemical properties enables the development of efficient miniaturized biosensor systems with simple usability.

Lectins are currently being extensively explored in the field of biosensors as biorecognition molecules because of their specific interaction properties with free or conjugated carbohydrate residues, which is of interest for detecting bacteria in human serological samples [15]. Concanavalin A (Con A) is a type of lectin with specificity for glucose/mannose and can differentiate glycoproteins present in serum from microorganism-infected patients. The lectin-based sensor has shown good sensitivity and potential for the development of new nanostructured platforms for microorganism diagnosis [10].

In this paper, we report the development of a new biosensor platform based on a self-assembled nanostructured film based on polypyrrole, QDGs, and lectin ConA chemically modified (Fig. S1), for residues detection of glucose-mannose on surfaces of C. albicans, C. Glabata, C. tropicalis, E. coli, P. aeruginosa, B. subtilis, and S. aureus. The proposed biosensor platform was effective for the diagnosis of important microorganisms involved in biomedical infections.

Indium tin oxide (ITO) coated polyethylene terephthalate (PET), Pyrrole (98 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %), graphene quantum dots (1 mg/mL), Concanavalin A lectin, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-[3-dimethylaminopropyl] carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and bovine serum albumin (BSA, ∼98 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Ferric chloride (≥99 %) and ferrous chloride (≥98.7 %) were obtained from Vetec (Duque de Caxias, RJ, Brazil). Microorganisms were obtained from the Department of Tropical Medicine/UFPE. The material collected from patient samples was provided by Prof. Reginaldo Gonçalves, with authorization from the UFPE ethics committee. The reagents and solvents used will be of analytical grade.

The first step towards construction involved the electropolymerization of polypyrrole (PPy) on the flexible ITO (indium tin oxide) electrode. Indium tin oxide (ITO) coated polyethylene terephthalate (PET) with a surface resistivity of 60 Ω/sq was used for the development of the flexible electrodes. The flexible electrode was immersed in a 0.5 M hydrochloric acid solution and 30 mM pyrrole monomer. Using the cyclic voltammetry technique, six polymerization cycles occurred in a potential range of −0.4 to +1 V, with a scan rate of 100 mV/s. After this step, 5 μL of graphene quantum dots (0.1 mg/mL) were absorbed into the PPy layer for 10 minutes. Then, 2 μL EDC:NHS (0.4:0.1 M, v/v) solution was added to the electrode surface for 20 minutes to promote the chemical bond between graphene quantum dots and ConA lectin. 5 μL of the ConA (0.5 mg/ mL, PBS 10 mM) was immobilized on the surface of the PPy-QDGs layer for 15 minutes, after the aforementioned EDC:NHS-mediated activation. A solution of 2 μL of 1 % (w/v) bovine serum albumin (BSA) was used for 30 seconds to block the remaining active sites. All stages of biosensor construction were characterized using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques.

Microorganisms from the Tropical Diseases Diagnostic Laboratory were characterized by Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization Time-of-Flight Mass Spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS). The analyses were performed on an Axima LNR system (Kratos Analytical, Shimadzu, UK) equipped with a nitrogen laser (337 nm), where the laser intensity was set just above the threshold for ion production. All solutions were freshly prepared and stored at 5 ºC during the work. To assess the quality and reproducibility of the spectral data, each yeast isolate was analysed in triplicate. All samples were air dried at room temperature and finally analysed by MALDI-TOF MS. For the correct identification of the microorganisms evaluated through MALDI-TOF MS, the following steps were followed: a) application of the samples on the ionization matrix, b) high-power laser ionization, c) time-of-flight (TOF) analysis, d) spectra mass obtention, and e) evaluation of the generated mass spectra. The bacteria (Bacillus subtilis, Escherichia coli, and Staphylococcus aureus) were cultivated in nutrient agar. Yeasts (Candida albicans, Candida tropicalis, and Candida glabata) were cultivated and identified using mass spectrometry. Incubations were standardized, and strains were grown aerobically at 37 ºC. The inoculum preparation followed the McFarland 0.5 standard and was standardized in a spectrophotometer (Spectronic Genesys, Application SoftcardTM, Milton Roy) for subsequent tests. Ethical Approval was obtained by the institutional ethics committee (number 4.207.133). For the biorecognition tests of the biosensor against microorganisms, five saline suspensions were prepared at concentrations of 101, 102, 103, 104, and 105 CFU/mL. Subsequently, 5 µL of these suspensions were applied to the biosensor surface and incubated for 15 minutes. After, the sensor surface was rinsed with deionized water, and electrochemical measurements, including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), were performed.

Electrochemical analyses were performed using a PGSTAT 128 N potentiostat/galvanostat (Autolab, Netherlands). CV measurements were performed at a scan rate of 50 mV/s in a range of potentials between −0.2 and +0.7 V. EIS was obtained from 100 mHz to 100 kHz, with an amplitude of the applied sine wave potential of 10 mV. Electrochemical measurements were carried out in a conventional three-electrode electrochemical cell, platinum wire as auxiliary electrode, Ag/AgCl (saturated with KCl) electrode as reference electrode, and ITO electrode as working electrode, housed inside a Faraday cage, in the presence of a 10 mM (K4[Fe(CN)6]4−/K3[Fe(CN)6]3−) used as a redox probe. All measurements were performed in triplicate using three different samples (n=3). The reproducibility of the ConA Biosensor was calculated through the standard deviation of voltammetric assays from three biosensors constructed separately using the same experimental procedure. Repeatability was estimated using the relative standard deviation (RSD) of biorecognition assays at a concentration of 101 CFU/mL, employing the equation: RSD (%) = (Standard deviation / Average) x 100.

Topographic analyses were performed with an SPM9700 atomic force microscope (Shimadzu Instruments Co. Ltd, Japan) in a non-contact mode at room temperature (∼ 25 °C). Cantilevers with silicon AFM probes (Nanoworld, Japan, resonant frequency = 190 kHz, spring constant = 42 N/m) were used. The images (512 points per line) were collected with a scan rate of 1.0 Hz in a scan area of 5 × 5 µm. Gwyddion software was used for analysis and processing of the images.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was conducted with a Vega 3XM Tescan instrument at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. The UV–vis characterization was performed using the spectrophotometerKasvi K37-UVVIS in the wavelength range from 200 nm to 800 nm in transparent quartz cells.

In the three-dimensional study of the biosensor assembly steps, each layer exhibited intrinsic characteristics related to the material used in self-assembly. In Fig. 1a, we observe the layer corresponding to the electropolymerized PPy film on the ITO flexible electrode surface. The characteristic behavior of this polymer is evident, presenting a nodular aspect and a maximum surface peak of 0.14 µm Ra = 19 nm and RMS = 25.92 nm), consistent with previous works [16]. With the adsorption of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a noticeable topographical change occurs on the surface, showing a significant increase in the maximum surface peak to 0.16 µm. Additionally, there is an elevation in the roughness average (Ra) to 24.23 nm and root mean square (RMS) to 31.58 nm. This surface heterogeneity is associated with the formation of nanomaterial aggregates on the PPy (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, in the characterization by scanning electron microscopy of PPy coated with GQDs, the nodular aspect (Fig. S2a) and the flakes of the quantum dots adhered to the surface were observed (Fig. S2b) [17,18]. Moreover, the GQDs were also characterized by UV–vis, where the maximum absorption peak between 325 and 400 nm related to the size of the quantum dots dispersed in deionized water was identified (Fig. S2c). Upon immobilization of ConA on GQDs, the maximum surface peak reaches 0.25 µm, and there is an increase of 13.01 nm in the average surface roughness Ra = 37.24 nm and RMS = 40.15 nm). This profile results from the covalent immobilization directed by the EDC/NHS agent, keeping ConA on the surface of GQDs (Fig. 1c) [10]. In the last construction step with the addition of bovine serum albumin (BSA), the sensor surface exhibits a maximum peak of 0.38 µm, confirming the self-assembly of the platform Ra = 53.4 nm and RMS = 61.32 nm) (Fig. 1d). BSA is used to block nonspecific sites due to its ability to interact on cationic surfaces [19]. When the ConA biosensor is exposed to a bacterial suspension of E. coli, the maximum surface peak is elevated to 0.53 µm, withRa = 90.9 nm and RMS = 111.1 nm (Fig. 1e). Similarly, exposure to a fungal suspension of C. albicans elevates the maximum surface peak to 0.61 µm Ra = 96.3 nm and RMS = 127.6 nm) (Fig. 1f). This behavior indicates the binding of these microorganisms to the surface, demonstrating the ConA lectin's recognition capacity [20].

The study began with an examination of the ideal electropolymerization cycle for PPy. Electropolymerization of PPy offers significant advantages compared to other techniques due to its stability, thickness control, polymer oxidation, and the achievement of an active electrocatalytic film [21]. Thus, the voltammetric response of the film obtained in 6 and 20 electropolymerization cycles was investigated (Figs. S3a and S2b). The PPy film formed with 20 cycles exhibited lower peak currents than those formed with 6 cycles (Figs. S3c and S3d). This behavior is associated with monomer consumption in the medium, leading to changes in the thickness and conductivity of the film's outer layers [22]. Consequently, the 6th electropolymerization cycle was deemed ideal due to its stability and higher values of anodic and cathodic peak currents in cyclic voltammetry (CV). This choice aligns with the pattern established in previous works [16]. In the second stage of biosensor construction, GQDs were adsorbed onto the PPy surface. This adsorption is attributed to the interaction between the positively charged cationic polymer and the negatively charged carboxylic groups of the quantum dots [18,23]. A study of adsorption time was conducted to determine the ideal parameter for construction. It was observed that longer GQDs adsorption times resulted in a greater decline in anodic and cathodic currents in CV assays (Fig. S4). Thus, a 15-minute adsorption time was selected as ideal since it does not cause a significant loss of amperometric signal and allows the formation of a satisfactory nanostructured layer.

After standardizing the PPy-GQDs nanostructuration, all steps were characterized using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The anodic and cathodic peak currents from the CV assays can be found in Table S1. The EIS assays were quantitatively assessed using the Randles circuit as the equivalent circuit of the platform, where the components were solution resistance (RS), constant phase element (CPE), charge transfer resistance (RCT), and Warburg impedance (W) (Fig S5). The values can be visualized in Table S2.

In the cyclic voltammetry evaluation, it was observed that PPy presented broad current peaks (IPA = 280.78 ± 28.03 µA and IPC = - 247.49 ± 22.68 µA) ( Fig. 2a). This behavior is expected from a conductive polymer film with high electron transfer and stability [21]. In the EIS assays, no significant semicircle formation in the high-frequency region of the Nyquist diagram was observed (RCT = 31.23 ± 1.31 Ω) (Fig. 2b). This result indicates low resistance to charge transfer between the polymeric surface and the electroanalytical solution [24].

In the second layer of structuring with GQDs, a decrease in IPA and IPC occurred because the negative portion of the redox pair in the electroanalytical solution was repelled by the negative charges of the -COOH in the nanostructures (IPA = 255 ± 39.15 µA and IPC = - 258 ± 40 µA). In the EIS assay, this behavior is reflected in the appearance of a semicircle in the impedance spectrum and an increase in RCT (RCT = 65.62 ± 2.29 Ω) (Fig. 2b). These findings corroborate with previous studies [18].

In the third assembly step, ConA lectin was immobilized. Carboxylic groups of GQDs were activated using the EDC/NHS agent. Covalent bonds formed between the -NH groups available in the protein chain and the activated -COOH groups of the quantum dots. In the electrochemical characterization by CV, the coating of the PPy-QGDs surface with ConA was evidenced by the decrease in current peaks in the CV assays (IPA = 228.94 ± 3.26 µA and IPC = - 213.87 ± 2.57 µA) (Fig. 2a). Inversely, there was an increase in the semicircle diameter in the EIS (RCT = 170 ± 17.3 Ω) (Fig. 2b). This response is due to the partial blockage of electron transfer from the surface by the protein chain.

Finally, bovine serum albumin was used to block nonspecific sites on the surface. This blocking resulted in a slight decrease in voltammetric response (IPA = 200.44 ± 1.91 µA and IPC = - 227.9 ± 0.5 µA) (Fig. 2a) and an increase in the impedimetric response (RCT = 243 ± 5.7 Ω) (Fig. 2b).

The ConA biosensor was assessed against diverse microorganisms, as follows: Bacillus subtilis, Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Candida albicans, Candida tropicalis, and Candida glabrata. A decrease in peak currents was observed in cyclic voltammetry (CV) assays with different Candida spp. species, corresponding to an increase in CFU ( Fig. 3a, c, and e). This process occurs due to the interaction of ConA with the yeast cell walls. ConA lectin can recognize and bind to specific carbohydrate residues with high affinity [10]. An increase in the Nyquist diagrams was observed following the yeast concentration, corroborating the voltammetric results (Fig. 3b, d, and f). Candida spp. have a high proportion of carbohydrates in their cell walls, such as d-glucose (Glc), N-acetyl-d-glucosamine (GlcNAc), and d-mannose (Man) [25]. Thus, ConA lectin can bind effectively to glucose and mannose residues on the microorganism surface. Similar electrochemical behaviors were observed in the CV ( Fig. 4a, c, and e) and EIS (Fig. 4b, d, and f) measurements.

These electrochemical changes are associated with the formation of the ConA-bacteria recognition complex. ConA lectin binds to the bacterial wall by recognizing glucose and mannose saccharide residues on the bacterial surface. This binding leads to the attachment of bacteria to the sensor surface, reducing the access of the redox couple to the electrode surface and decreasing the voltammetric response with increasing sample concentration. The biorecognition enhances the impedance response of the sensor system. This behavior is observed quantitatively as the RCT varies with the concentration of the evaluated microorganisms.

Furthermore, it is noticeable that each microorganism exhibited a distinct electrochemical behavior for the same concentration range (101 – 105 CFU/mL). The bacteria showed lower anodic peak currents and higher values of RCT even at the lowest concentration levels compared to Candida spp. Among the fungi, C. tropicalis showed a decrease in oxidation and reduction currents, which was reflected in the increase of RCT. The peak currents can be visualized in Table S3, and the RCT values in Table S4. These results confirm the possibility of ConA biorecognition against microorganisms of distinct natures. The surface carbohydrates of bacteria can vary mainly with host interaction, environmental conditions, and antibiotic exposure. The ability to cause damage to the host and colonization is closely linked to immune system evasion, and these factors are closely linked to the polysaccharide surface of bacteria, which can prevent phagocytosis by phagocytic cells [26]. These factors indicate a well-developed strategy for detecting and differentiating microorganisms using lectins such as ConA.

In the study of response differentiation, the percentage surface coverage (θ%) of the sensor surface during biorecognition was considered. θ% was calculated using the equation: θ% = [1 - (RCT Biosensor / RCT microorganism) x 100]. RCT and θ% values can be found in Table S4. At a concentration of 101 CFU/mL, we observed different results for the yeasts, in the following sequence: C. tropicalis (θ% = 34.7 % ± 0.4), C. albicans (θ% = 12.1 % ± 2.7), and C. glabrata (θ% = 3.5 % ± 4.7) ( Fig. 5a and b). This distinction is attributed to differences in the surface carbohydrate profiles among Candida species. Additionally, the variability in the content and configuration of mannose on the surface of C. albicans may result in adhesion inhibition, serving as an evasion mechanism from the immune system [27].

A subtle distinction in θ% at 101 UFC/mL occurred in the differential evaluation of bacterial strains. Gram-positive bacteria, B. subtilis (θ% = 66.4 % ± 0.4), and S. aureus (θ% = 59.1 % ± 0.6) demonstrated greater surface coverage, while the gram-negative bacterium (E. coli: θ% = 47.4 % ± 2.5) exhibited lower coverage (Fig. 5a and c). Thus, the recognition order was B. subtilis > S. aureus > E. coli. It was observed that the Gram-positive bacteria (B. subtilis, and S. aureus) showed a greater impedimetric response than E. coli, a Gram-negative bacterium. This finding may be related to the greater carbohydrate residues found on Gram-positive bacteria's surface than Gram-negative ones [28]. Another factor that may have influenced this electrochemical response is the microorganism's size. The larger the layer formed on the sensor surface, the greater the partial blockage of electron transfer between the biosensor and the electroanalytical solution. This process impacts RCT values and is reflected in surface coverage [29]. B. subtilis, with a size of approximately 4 – 10 µm [30], showed a greater impedimetric response. A similar impedimetric response occurred between S. aureus and E. coli, which have sizes ranging from 0.1 – 1.5 µm and 1 – 1.5 µm, respectively [31] .

The bacteria biorecognition assays showed elevated surface coverage values on the biosensor surface, while Candida spp. exhibited intermediate values. Thus, the electrochemical response allows the differentiation between these microorganisms, reflecting the carbohydrates' binding affinity. Within the Candida spp. group, distinct IPA, RCT, and surface coverage values were also observed, enabling classification based on the obtained response amplitude. Conversely, the response amplitudes within the studied bacteria group were close, making exact distinction of specieschallenging. Nevertheless, a differential response in the range of 101 – 103 CFU/mL was still possible. Thus, the proposed biosensor can differentiate between Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, showing higher analytical sensitivity for S. aureus and B. subtilis (Fig. 5).

As a final overview, the biosensor can differentiate Candida species and bacteria from each other. However, the proposed biosensor detects bacteria without clear differentiation among them. The limits of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LQ) obtained were 1.42 and 2.3 CFU/mL for C. albicans, with a linear regression equation of y = 0.079 + 0.16 log(x) and R2 = 0.97. C. glabrata obtained LOD = 3.72 CFU/mL and LQ = 5.59 CFU/mL, with a linear equation of y = 0.008 + 0.15 log(x) and R2 = 0.92. The study of C. tropicalis yielded an LOD of 0.18 CFU/mL and an LQ of 0.13 CFU/mL, with a linear equation of y = 0.278 + 0.071 log(x) and R2 = 0.99. The respective LODs and LQs for the evaluated bacteria were 0.39 and 0.14 CFU/mL for S. aureus (y = 0.556 + 0.035 log(x) and R2 = 0.99), LOD = 0.72 CFU/mL and LQ = 0.145 CFU/mL for B. subtilis (y = 0.645 + 0.018 log(x) and R2 = 0.99), and LOD of 2.63 CFU/mL and LQ of 1.29 CFU/mL for E. coli (y = 0.457 + 0.049 log(x) and R2 = 0.94). The ConA biosensor exhibited a reproducibility of 1.91 % and repeatability of 21 % for Candida spp. and 27 % for bacteria.

To evaluate the specificity of the ConA-Biosensor, a saline solution containing 50 mM galactose and no microorganisms was used as a negative control. A slight increase in anodic and cathodic peaks was observed when the biosensor was exposed to the negative control for 30 minutes. This response was different from the results obtained when testing with microorganisms in the same suspension (Fig. S6). To calculate the overall specificity of the biosensor, three repetitions of the negative control were performed on different days with three separately constructed ConA-biosensors. Specificity was calculated as: Specificity = [True Negative Results / (True Negative Results + False Positive Results)] x 100. Accordingly, the ConA-Biosensor demonstrated a specificity of 90 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biotechnological application of lectins in microbiological biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: Given the critical need for rapid detection of microorganisms, particularly within hospital environments, several microbiological biosensors have been developed [32]. Additionally, considering the biotechnological potential of lectins, these proteins are widely employed as bioreceptors [33]. Table 1 provides a comparison of various studies utilizing lectins for microbiological diagnosis. Sá et al. developed an impedimetric biosensor on a gold electrode using ConA and wheat germ lectin (WGA) to detect and differentiate Candida species, achieving a sensitive platform with a LOD of 102 CFU/mL [10]. Oliveira et al. utilized Cramoll lectin to recognize glucose and manose in Candida spp. through fluorescence microscopy and quantum dots [34]. Silva et al. employed ConA to develop an electrochemical biosensor capable of detecting and differentiating between gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria, with an LOD of 101 CFU/mL [26]. Punjabi et al. detected bacteria through an optical biosensor based on WGA lectin and nanoparticles with an LOD of 105 CFU/mL [35].

This study utilized flexible ITO strips to construct the ConA biosensor on a distinct substrate and innovative nanostructured platform, demonstrating a potential for miniaturization. Flexible ITO (indium tin oxide), a PET (polyethylene terephthalate) coated with indium tin oxide is a promising material in the development of sensors that has significant electrochemical properties. The ITO substrate offers flexibility, electrochemical stability, transparency, and compatibility with large-scale manufacturing processes. Finding useful substrates for the portability and miniaturization of biodevices developed to replace conventional substrates, such as gold and carbon electrodes, continues to be the subject of study. In addition, The flexibility of the material facilitates the integration of the biosensor into fabrics, plastics and coatings, enabling the creation of portable devices that can be easily incorporated into hospital environments. Furthermore, the proposed nanostructured biosensor exhibited excellent analytical sensitivity with low LODs. Compared to screen-printed electrodes, this substrate has advantages as it is used directly and immediately after the electrode-cutting process, requiring no spray coating or additional curing or cleaning process. Furthermore, the proposed nanostructured biosensor exhibited excellent analytical sensitivity with low LODs. The proposed biosensor has a lower cost, rapid detection, and high analytical sensitivity (Table S5) as compared to gold-standard methods (PCR or MALDI-TOF MS).

The ConA biosensor has been developed on a flexible and electroactive substrate, providing a greater potential for miniaturization. ITO substrate offers compatibility with large-scale manufacturing processes, active electrochemical properties, and ease of use. Moreover, using materials such as PPy and GQDs has contributed to the electroanalytical signal, resulting in increased sensitivity in the system. The proposed biosensor demonstrated the ability to differentiate between fungi of the Candida spp. and bacteria. The biosensor showed higher analytical sensitivity in detecting Candida spp. The greater analytical sensitivity (LOD of 0.18 CFU/mL) was obtained for C. tropicalis. Also, it was possible to distinguish between the species C. tropicalis, C. albicans, and C. glabrata with high sensitivity. The ConA biosensor exhibited higher analytical sensitivity in detecting Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus and B. subtilis). The differential response obtained for the sensor can be attributed to the heterogeneous distribution of carbohydrates on the microorganism's surfaces. The proposed system based on a flexible substrate is effective for microbiological diagnosis.

Maria D.L. Oliveira: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. César A.S. Andrade: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Reginaldo G. Lima-Neto: Methodology, Investigation. Léony S. de Oliveira: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Maria P.M.C. Santos: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Andrade would like to thank the Brazilian National Council of Scientific and Technological Development/CNPq (grant numbers 304678/2021–0 and 304680/2021–4) and Foundation for Science and Technology of the State of Pernambuco. Maria P. M. C. Santos and Léony S. de Oliveira would like to thank Professor H.P. Oliveira for his support in scanning electron microscopy analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jpba.2024.116299.
